speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Beulah Dollar Herman took place at her home in Troy on November 9, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I guess it was 28 and we've lived in Moscow and here all this time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came, where were you coming from?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: We came from Colorado out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that made you decide?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, my husband wanted to get out on a farm someplace. We were living on a farm in Colorado. They had so many windstorms there. It was hard on the crops and he wanted to get out here. He knew some people that lived in Moscow, the Bongarars, years and years ago. I didn't know them. He came out here and looked around and he liked the country. The following year we moved out and we've been here ever since. As I said, I can't remember just when I started to work for Psychiana. I see it was, must have been about 1943 or four that I worked there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you worked before you worked there? Had you worked around in Moscow?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No. I just worked there.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Because it was a type of work, about the only type of work they had around Moscow for women was working, sorting the peas. So many people couldn't do that because they run on a belt and you couldn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Working with - Why? They couldn't do it because it was hard?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: It made them sick. It was a movement all the time, you see. That's why so many of them worked for Dr. Roberson because it was a type of work they could do and it wasn't hard. It was a little bit confining and sometimes monotonous. But then it was a job. As I said, he was very nice to work for. Of course, he very seldom ever come around to pay too much attention about the work. Of course, we always saw him. His office was in the building where I worked. As I said, I saw Dr. Bach when he was there. He was there two or three times while he was in Moscow and Dr. Roberson brought him out and introduced him to all of us. Then after this book came out, he gave each other the book.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I think that you got - I'm pretty saying that most of the people that worked there had a very positive impression of him. He thought he was a very good person.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, all of us that worked there definitely thought - and as I said, he was very nice. It was more of the town people that were more or less down on him, but he never hurt anyone. He never did and he done a lot of good for the town. He is the one that put the post office on first -class rate. He did that with all of his mailing. You see, I worked in the mailing department. All of our building was advertisement that we sent out the mail for these lessons. Then when the lessons - anyone that wanted to fill in the cards and send them back, they went to the other building and then they mailed the lessons out. Any time there was a fundraising deal in Moscow and they were short, Dr. Robinson filled it out. It was never published because he didn't care for that publicity. As I said, most of us knew it, but the people in town never giving credit for anything. They were a lot of people in town.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Probably in this one drug store there, he mentions him in one of his books in the corner drug. He helped him when he first began getting started there. There was a few of them that put money in that had a little interest in it and they cut their money back and everything. But his time went on while he didn't need any help. This was when he first started. But as I said, Dr. Robinson never hurt anybody. You could get a lot of good out of his lessons if you wanted to follow through with him. Of course, now none of us working there ever had them because that was one regulation.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Very seldom did any of us ever read the advertisements. Of course, they changed from time to time. In the hours that we worked, we weren't allowed to be reading it. We were supposed to be working. If some of us wanted to take some of it home, we did. I used to take some of it home, but it's been so long ago, I don't even remember what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you couldn't take the lessons home and look at them?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, now the lessons were put out from the other building.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Were there any of them ever taking any of them home?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I wouldn't know. But in our mail, it was a regulation because we mailed thousands and thousands of letters a day. We'd see we had to sort them just like they do in the post office. And if there was any from Moscow, we threw them out. Moscow to Poland, town, we threw them out. That was a regulation. Because, you see, he knew that the people of Moscow didn't go along with him on his beliefs, so he didn't bother him.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, I guess one thing that I had heard was that he was afraid that he would be accused of trying to take people away from the other denominations. Yes, yes, that was part of it.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: As I said, he never interfered with any of the churches up there or anything else. But as I said, he just didn't bother him at all. But they were a well -fought -of family, and I knew all of the family very well.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But he discouraged you, the people that worked with him, from looking at his lessons or looking at him from being a part of the religion, too. He didn't really want the people. Of course, he never did tell us that.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But then the supervisors, you know, over it, they always, when they heard anyone, as a rule, lots of the girls would come in there. I knew when they heard them, that sometimes they didn't tell them that, but every once in a while you'd see them reading it. And any of us that had worked there for a while, we'd just go and tell them to go ahead with their business in hand, what they're supposed to do, that they weren't supposed to. If they wanted to read them, they'd take them home whether or not it's while it's working. And of course, if one of the supervisors had seen it, she'd always come over and lull them out. And the rest of us would do this on the slide to keep them from getting a bowling out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how were the supervisors to work for? What were the working conditions like? How much was expected of all of you as far as output and all that?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: They expected us to do an awful lot.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: That is, if we put out 35 ,000 a day, they thought we should put out 40 or 50 a day. And of course, that always depended on how many of us was working on the mailers, because you could only do so much of that. But then, now one of the supervisors is still living. She lives there in Moscow on Hay Street, Jenny Nesbeth. Yeah, if you wanted to, of course, her husband isn't very well. And so I don't know if you stopped there some time. But then she is still living, and she's the one that I worked under all the time I worked there. Of course, there were some others that worked closer to her, too, you see. If she couldn't be there, there were others supervisors taking it over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was in charge of how many of the whole operation in the building?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No, she was in charge of our building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which was?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: See, in the back of our building was where all the machines were, where they had done all the printing and the folding. And then they were brought in from there into our part of the building. And we had to, oh, most of the time we had four or five different folding things that we put in an envelope. And then you see they were put in these envelopes that already had the names on them. And then when we got so many hundreds and hundreds of them, part of us had to go and work on the mailers and sort them all out. And it had to be done and got to the post office each day so they could be sent out. And we run them through those mailers, as I said, as lots of times said, I know that we mailed as high as 65 ,000 and 75 ,000 a day. Of course, we really had to work fast to do it. But then, lots of times, we didn't put that many out because sometimes when we had to change, each group of names went by numbers. And when you got those filled, well, you started in on another group and they all had to be kept separate, you see, until they were put into the mailer. So that way, when the cards were numbered that were put in there, they'd come back to the other building and they could tell what percent of a certain group of advertising names, what percent they got back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your own job there? What were you doing?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, I did a little bit of all in that part because none of us ever worked in the part of the building where the machinery was. We had, I stamped cards, as I said, they had these cards that you filled out. But each group of advertising names that you got, like they come from this company in New York, this company in Chicago, or someplace else, they were kept separate. And if there was 40 ,000 of them names, you stamped 40 ,000 cards with that number on them. And that was one of my jobs. But you see, you would get tired of doing that because there was only a few of us that could stamp those cards because you had to do it fast. Well, and then, as I said, we had these stacks of folded material in front of us. Sometimes it was three or four and then a card. And you'd put them together like this and put them in an envelope. And then we'd put them in these boxes. And when you got these boxes full, they were about this size, but they went to the mailer and then they had to be run through the mailer.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And the information in those envelopes would be just a general, mostly in advertisements for course, IKEA. And the card then, if you wanted the lessons, you filled the card out and mailed them back. And then they went up to the other building. And they sent the lessons out to the other building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These lists that he got, were they from, how did he get them? Were they from organizations or where were they from?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: A lot of people yet today wonder how they get an advertisement in the mail. Well, it's very easy for me to know because that was one of my jobs. I opened up those boxes by the hundreds and hundreds. One advertisement company will sell them to maybe 5 ,000 other companies. Like if there's an ad in a paper for something, regardless of what it is, and you know how many people may send in for it. They've got your name on the mailing list. They sell that list of names.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I said, if there's 5 ,000 other companies that want them, they sell them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you have any idea of where his lists were coming from?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, I know where they all came from. But of course, as I said, it was just everywhere. Maybe it was somebody that was advertising some foolish, ridiculous thing to sell. It's like I said, Dr. Robison's, what Dr. Robison put out didn't hurt anybody with some of this stuff, where they got the names at. Well, I wonder if he got a list from seed companies or something like that. Anyplace. Anywhere he could, that really. Any advertisement that you see in any magazine, they have the right to sell those names. And they do sell them. They sell them to hundreds and hundreds of advertising. And yet today, you know, like you can get something in the mail that you didn't send for or anything, and you wonder where it come from. That's where it come from. It was sold from some place that has your name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you, when we talked a little bit about the kind of people who would be interested in the lessons, did you have any idea yourself on who would be the people who were asking for the psychiatric?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, I never met any of them, no. As I said, they were from every place else in the United States and in the world. You see, we had, we mailed a lot of them to different places in Europe, different islands. And, as I said, no, I've never met any of the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that a great part of the people who were getting the material were saved from the big cities? Was what? Were saved from the big cities? Do you think many of them?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: An awful lot of them, yes. An awful lot of them.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But, of course, not all. But they had to be, we could tell that, because now, like, when we went through the list of New York City names, you know, that part of the mailer would be full. Five, six times a day. There's Chicago or San Francisco, or all like that. But then, of course, they were little towns, too, because in sorting your mail, unless you had ten from one town, they had to go separately, you know. So, as I said, but it's been a good many years since I worked there, a lot of it I forgot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what makes you think that the lessons, what's your own personal knowledge to feel that the lessons were good for people?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I think so, because it's like I said, they wouldn't hurt anybody if you wanted to follow through with them. And, there was a course of lessons, I can't remember any more how many, they were set, and then they were followed up. And, any time, if they wanted to discontinue, if they could, or if they wanted their money back, they got it back. That is, if they hadn't got all the lessons. But then, outside of that, that's all I really know about the lessons, because that wasn't in our department.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the cost of them?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No, I used to know, yes, but I didn't. I haven't run across it in the literature. No, I don't think you would in any of that. Now, you'll like in some of this literature, because Lonnie said that he knew that all of his writings and literature that he had at home after he passed away, you see, then she moved from the house sometime afterwards. And, he knew that it was turned over to the university. And, some of the books, he thought that hadn't been put out or something like that. He said all of that was turned over there. And, he said, at that time, when I told him about you, he said, well, I'd like to talk to him. And, I said, well, you bring the book up, and I said, we'll give him a ring. But, as I said, you know, he kept forgetting to bring it up, because he was busy working down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the pay like that you got for working there, do you remember?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Not very much, because that was during the Depression that I worked there. And, when I started in, I was getting sixty -some dollars a month. But, that was pretty good pay at that time, you know. And, of course, we got a few raises. But, there was one thing that he never done. If we were out a few days sick, we were never done.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: We were paid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? You mean you got paid for those days?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: We got paid for it, yes. We did.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But, as I said, that was during hard times, and it isn't like your salaries. Sure. No. But, that was pretty good pay for. Of course, now the girls that did the typing, that did typing, they got more pay. And, of course, the supervisors got more pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have breaks in your workday?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Fifteen minutes is all. Once a day. Once a day. Because that has been changed. Because, at that time, people didn't have coffee breaks all day long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that fifteen minutes for lunch?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No. Well, we had an hour at lunch. But, we had fifteen minutes. We could either take it in the morning, or we could take it in the afternoon. And, only two of us went out together. And, that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the others kept working?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yeah, the others kept working. But, you went out from pairs. Yeah.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But, as I said, it isn't like it has been for the past twenty years. They get coffee breaks all the time. And, you were expected to put out a lot while you were working. Yes, we were. To work very fast. Yes, we worked. We was expected to put out a lot, and we did. What I mean is, like some days where there was some changeover with a lot of different numbers, or, of course, we didn't always run with as many. Sometimes, they'd be maybe twenty of us working, and then maybe they'd be kind of a slack period, and part of them would be laid off. But, there has been as many as forty -five or fifty working in the two buildings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that include the people working on the printing as well?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Of course, there's a rule that was only for in the printing, but then, they run those all day long. The printing machines and the folding machines, you know. And, a lot of the girls that worked there, they were girls that were putting their husbands through school, through the university. And, they had very little to live on, you know, but they were glad to have the work while they were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eight hours a day, five days a week? What? Eight hours a day, five days a week? Is that what it was?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were some of the people, were many of the people also local people, like yourself?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yeah, they were the paper mark.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: They were all local people right there. A few of them, a few of the girls came from Derry and a few of them from Troy. And, once in a while, you got some from Pullman, but most all of them were local people right there. Yeah, they all were, and they were all that. I do know that one girl that worked there, her small brother was killed out on the highway, and the mother was with her, and they didn't have very much. And, I do know that he paid all the funeral expenses and everything for it, but the people in town never knew that. They didn't know it. As I said, he didn't want them to know those things. And, as I said, other than that, I do know that when they were shorting funds, when they were raising funds for things, if they were short, he made it up. But, he never got any credit for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think that the people who disliked him in Moscow, why do you think there was so much of this giving him a hard time?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: That would be only in my own opinion. So, many of them, Moscow is a town that is a little bit hard to get used to. I've always felt that, years and years back, that they always felt a little above most of other towns, or something, why I wouldn't know. But, I think most of them were just more or less down on him, because they didn't believe in what he was doing. But, if you pinned them right down to it, they didn't even know what he was doing. And, as I said, most of it, because I've very seldom ever talked to anybody about it, because if anybody, if I was around somebody, and they started saying something about him, I'd always just say, well, let's drop it right there. I work for Dr. Robinson, and he's a very fine man to work for, and you don't know anything about what you're talking about.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And, so, that's who it was.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And I know he set up a youth center up there. There was no place for the younger school kids to go to, and he put up this youth center. And, every bit of it, he paid for every bit of it, and paid to have it kept open, and paid to have someone stay there. He did that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it free to the kids?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes. It was free for the kids. He did that. And, another thing, there's, where Robinson's Lake is, in Moscow, that he'd give that to the county. And, for years, it was never anything much done about it. They didn't try to keep it up. But, now, in the past ten or so years, they have tried to do something with it. Of course, it's like a lot of other parts and things. They try to fix up something, and then it's destroyed. But, now, Dr. Robinson did that, too. And, I don't know of anybody else around town that did those things. That's right. And, as I said, that is my opinion, and it was most everyone's opinion. Of course, we ourselves that worked there, we didn't discuss those things, you know.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: It's interesting what you say about Moscow as being a little cold, because other people who came to Moscow in the twenties have told me the same thing. It was not an easy town to be accepted in. No, it isn't. It isn't.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And, we lived up there twenty -some years, twenty -three years. And, so, I felt that I, you know, with what I'd say, really, that's the way Moscow is. Of course, now, in the past ten, fifteen, twenty years, there's been lots of other people come in. But, believe me, a lot of those older people up there are still just like that. They are not a bit friendly. They never were. And, as I said, they were against Dr. Robinson. As I said, of course, he didn't let it bother him any. But, he knew that that's the way they felt, so that's why the regulations were what they were. And, as I said, we always, we went by the regulations. We respected them, and we went by them. And, but there was a lot of us that worked there that we couldn't work anyplace else. Of course, back then, there was very few places in Moscow where you could work. Nowadays, you know, most of the wives can work up at the university in places like that, putting their husbands to the school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, Do you feel that there was something spiritual about him as a person?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, I do. Yes, I really do. Because, I think it had to then for him to do what he did. I really do. Of course, now, there again, the people of Moscow didn't think so. And, I can't truly remember any minister around Moscow that ever had anything to do with Dr. Robinson. And, me, myself, I don't think that's speaking very highly of the ministers up there then. Of course, there's none of them there now that were there. But, I, myself, don't think that was speaking very highly for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, would you?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No, I think you should have speak to him and spend time with him. I think so. But, it's it. It must have put him, even if it didn't bother him much, it must have, when people doubt you, it makes things more difficult for you. It just seems natural to me. If people think that you're, they suspect you, they don't trust you most, well, you have to feel it hurt. And, it has to make you feel isolated, at least. Yes. Well, now, he more or less was. Of course, as I said, he come and went without interfering with anybody, you know.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Dr. Robinson always drove some of the most expensive, well, the most expensive cars that were ever in Moscow. And, there again, I think a lot of people were jealous of him. And, there is a few people up there, years and years ago, that held up for him. But, they never, they never discussed him with anyone or anything. Because, that was just something that you didn't do. Because, everybody knew how the town was. And so, you just didn't. But, he come and went. Of course, he was a busy man. He was gone an awful lot. You can tell last time his books that you've read. Now, Mrs. Robinson, she belonged to some different clubs up there.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And, as I said, everyone always spoke very highly of her. I heard she was a very wonderful woman. That's what they did. And the kids. They did.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And, most of the boy and the girl was very nice. The girl Florence worked with us one summer. But, she did that. It was really a little funny. She, all through grade school, near in high school, she went with this boy that she finally married, Ral Ashby. And, one summer, the doctor and Mrs. Robinson and Al was going to spend the summer in California. And, Florence didn't want to go. And, he said, well, either you go or you go to work. Well, she said, then I'll go to work. So, he brought her down to the Psychiana building and introduced it to us. Of course, most of us never anyway. And, he said, now you put her to work. And, he told the supervisor, this Mrs. Nelson, said she doesn't get any favors at all. And, so, and she just loved working there. Of course, she didn't know a thing on earth about it. It was just like anyone that come in. But, you know, she was taught. And, she worked with us all. And, she really loved it. She loved every bit of it. And, when they came back then, and of course, she had to quit then when school started. So, she felt so bad about it because she was going to have to quit. So, she talked to folks in to having a big party, or it's up to their home. And, all of us women working there could invite our husbands and all of the husbands that worked in the other part. And, in both buildings, they could have their wives come. And, we had this big party after Dr. Robinson's house.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And, you feel that that was true of the of the group of women that worked there generally, that they were fairly close. It was a close social through. Yes, they were.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And then Florence went on and years went by and she and Raul got married. The last time I knew him, he was the captain, when he was retired. He was a retired captain from the Army.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: After they were married quite a while, because they had three children, and Florence had a polio. She did. But it didn't leave her very badly crippled. And I don't know where she and Raul is anymore. Of course, they were stationed on different islands and places. I don't know if they would come back now and then. But anyway, the doctor just felt that she'd always went with him. She never went with anyone else. He was a professor's son up there, a few of her children. But he was a good kid, you know. What I mean is, they put him to work down there, helping, doing a little bit of janitor work. And of course, he used to give all of us working there kind of a rough time, because he'd say, well, now you've got to all move. I'm going to do this. I've got to get out of here. And we'd say, now Raul, you know, you don't do any of this work until we are out of here. But of course, we'd go along with him. Anyway, the doctor really didn't want her to marry him, but they did. And as I said, he got to be a captain in the Army. And he is retired with a very big salary.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, you know, he figured that he couldn't find a boy good enough for his daughter. Yes, that's right. And I can remember so well, all quite some time before the doctor passed away, Al was supposed to come down and kind of learn the business a little bit.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And I remember the first time he'd come in, he'd come around to all of us, you know, we'd sit at these long tables, unless we was working on the mailer.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And he'd come around and stand by our chair, and he'd say, well, now, can you tell me just what you're doing?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And we would tell him... excuse me. Yes, he said that he was going to kind of look after some of the business a little bit, but he said, frankly, I don't know anything about it. So he said, if all of you can explain to me what you are doing, well, we did, as he come and talked to all of us. And of course, he did the same thing in the other building. And then it wasn't... he was down in the office at different times, but then we never knew too much about what he was doing. But then it was shortly after that that the doctor passed away. So then it wasn't too long after that that the manager and Al decided to just close up the business, and they did. And that I had quit working there by then when they closed it up. So that part of it. Jenny could tell you all about that, because she was still with them. About why they decided to... Yeah, because the thing was, once the doctor was gone, why I think that they felt that it really couldn't go on, because he was the one that always more or less kept the lessons going, and anything new that was put out, and none of them in the family could do that. And Mrs. Robinson had quite a bit of money in the newspaper there. It's out of onion now. At that time it was a news review. And then they just kept their interest in that, she and Al. But then Al was married too. And so as I said...

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, I had heard that his father wanted him to take over after he died, but that Al really couldn't do it. He didn't feel that he could, and he wanted to. He didn't feel that he knew, he didn't know anything about it.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: As I said, when he come in there, he told each of us, he said, I don't know a thing about it. He didn't know what we were doing. And when we showed him and told him, he still didn't understand it, you see, because his father had never told him anything about it. And as I said, I think after the... I know he would want him to go on with it, but I'm sure the manager... I can't remember who the manager was. It used to be Anderson. I forget what it is. Elmer Anderson. I think that's mentioned in some of these books. He's the one that I knew that was manager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's gone now, isn't he?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes. And I can't remember who... Oh, Cass was the one at the last... I can't remember his first name, but Cass. I think there is some of the Cass still living in Moscow, but he was. And as I said, Al didn't know only what little he'd found out, just being around there and worked some with his father before he passed away. And I don't think they felt that they knew enough about it to keep going.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And the children really were not involved in the running of the police. No, they weren't involved at all.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Mr. Robinson was completely sincere about his religion and the philosophy of life that he had through the religion. That Psychiana was a true faith for him. I think so. I don't think that he could have did what he did without believing in it.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And he was a very unusual man. And as I said, of course, he was a man that almost anyone could talk to. So many of these come from Dr. Bach and some of them that were so deeply interested in his work. It wasn't that any of them ever had his lessons in things. It was just that they were deeply interested in it. But as I said, I always felt that he had to be sincere to do what he did.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Wouldn't you think so?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, that's what's really struck me in reading the books that I have, is that they all seem to me to speak from his heart, that it's some deeply felt beliefs. And you see, what those people who didn't like him have had to say about him is that more or less that he was just trying to con people and that he wasn't sincere, that it was just set up as a big promotional deal. I'm sure you've heard that. That's the criticism.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And my own response to that is just, what you say, how could he have written what he wrote and the way he wrote it if he didn't feel what he was saying?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Because when you read it, it's just the feeling is there. Well, that's what I've always said.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And I'm sure with the lessons, they were sincere enough that there was thousands and thousands of people that had taken it. And if they hadn't have been that sincere, I don't think they could have ever went on through with them. And it's like I said, I happen to know that they wouldn't hurt anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your dad come?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: No.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But anyway, I always felt that he was sincere about it, but it's like you said, the people that were down on him, they said he wasn't. Well, it wasn't up to them to say. They didn't know. And I know a lot of people that was down on him, like some of the business people up there, if they thought what he was doing wasn't fair or was a little bit crooked, they themselves were on that side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On what side?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, they themselves weren't up and up. Oh. Because, as I said, regardless of what their business was, well, a lot of them wasn't. They wasn't doing things that were right. But they was down on him. And as I said, and another thing, part of them, I think, were jealous of him because he drove these expensive cars. They had expensive things. But to visit with them and talk with them, they were just as common as they could be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, they were just as common as they could be. Mrs. Robinson and him, too, to speak to him. Yes, he was. And he didn't go around town talking the rich into anybody. If anybody wanted to talk to him, well, that was fine. But he didn't hesitate to tell them what he thought. And that is why some of the ministers were down on him, because if some of the church people would tell him that they didn't believe this or believe that, like what he put out, well, he'd ask them how they could believe some of the stuff that was being talked to them. And, of course, as I said, they were all down on him. But I, myself, always felt that any of the ministers that was down on him, and they all were, and I didn't think that it was speaking very highly of them. And in my opinion, they just wasn't what I would call good Christian people, any of the ministers. And nearly always the Catholic priests, they come and went, of course, but they never would have anything to do with him, never. And I said, well, perhaps they're afraid he's going to take a little of his congregation away from him or something. But, no, I've always felt that Dr. Robinson, he was a very unusual man. He was an interesting man, and I've always felt that he was sincere about it all.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Of course, that would be my opinion.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that the people that worked there felt the same way?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, yes, they did. Of course, we had lots of help come and go. As I said, they were students, you know. Their husbands was going to university, and they were glad to have a job while they were there. And I met lots of them, and they come and went. They was from all over the United States. And, of course, you always got some that you had to let go. No, they couldn't keep up with the work and things. A lot of them, you know, it was just their paycheck was more than it was after. But then you find that with anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your husband farming at the time?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: He did at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And were there other women there whose husbands were farmers, too, working?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Oh, most of them lived there in town. We lived right off the edge of town, and we had to dairy for a while with the farm.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But most of them lived right there in town, and a lot of them were widowed when they needed the work and couldn't work other places. And, as I said, if you were sick a few days while you were never docked...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get raises while you were there? Did you get raises in pay while you were there?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, I think we had three or four raises. But they, of course, at that time, too, they didn't amount to too much. But, you know, it was a help. It isn't like it is nowadays or even 20 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you started about when? About in the early 40s, or was it even earlier than that?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I think it was about...

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: about in 43 and 4 and 5, somewhere along there. I think I still have some papers on some of my paychecks. When I was there, if I went to them, then I could tell.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But, as I said, I can't just remember exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember his bearing as being like Robinson's?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: His bearing, you know, the way he seemed was he... I heard he was pretty heavy. He was a large man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true? He was large and tall?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, he wasn't what you call a heavy man. Of course, he was tall, but he was big bones, you know. But his structure was... He was a very good -looking person.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And...

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But, as I said, now, he'd come and went. The office was in our building. He'd come and go. Sometimes we'd see him come in. The door was always locked until he'd come. And he'd open it and I am only in the experience.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: But... Out here, moved to out here by Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After living in Moscow, did you find the people to be friendlier?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Oh, my, my.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find much difference?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, there's all the difference in the world with the people around here.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Did you think so?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, I never lived in Moscow myself. No, that's right. You never lived there.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Where did you come from, to Troy?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I lived in Portland. But I was going to school there, so I wasn't exactly just living there. You know, it's kind of different. But I was wondering the contrast that, you know, if there was a real difference to you. Oh, yes, you bet there is. You bet there is. There's an awful lot of difference.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Of course, in a smaller town, of course, everybody knows one another here, you know, and everybody... And if anybody needs any help, you never saw a town that pitches in and helps like they do, always. If somebody's been in the hospital and had big bills, you know, hard luck and stuff, they put on all kinds of things to raise money for them. Everything. Or if anyone loses their home, why they just pitch right in and help them. And everybody does it. Of course, we're getting so many new people down here now. It isn't the same anymore. We've got four families right here from New Jersey.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Can you imagine that?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, there's four of them. I know part of them from New Jersey. And one of them, their father, one of them was out here this summer, and he was in the shop when I was. And he said, was asking about the, there wasn't quite a few people. And I said, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, really?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I said, I know there's nothing we can do about it, but I said, it is. We don't like it too well. He said, yes, but you still have lots of space here. I said, yes, but we're not going to have it this rate. And of course, I guess we do have a lot of space from where a lot of them are from. But at the rate they've been coming in here, you know, right now, I can go down on the little street. And half the people I meet, I don't know. It's just surprising. And it's the same way in all of these little towns.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Now, Post Falls, have you ever been up to Post Falls?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I've always felt that it was one of the nicest, quietest places. And now then, it has almost double in size, and they've got a problem up there. They sure do. Yeah, they sure have. And it's that way with so many of these. And you see, we don't have a school room for all of them anymore.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Well, you know, the difference that I was thinking of was in Moscow, you lived there for some years, and you still saw that kind of aloofness in some people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But here, when you moved out here, did you find that they just accepted you fairly fast, just right off?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Yes, that's right. That's really different. Right.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Because we didn't know, but, well, of course, you can't live this close to Troy without coming through it. In fact, we owned some land up towards Bovill, and we used to run cattle up there. Well, we'd always come through and like that. But as far as knowing the people here, we only knew of two families down this way. But then, you know, as I said, everybody is so friendly and everything. And of course, I always told everyone that Moscow was the most stuck -up town that I was ever in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think it was unusual for a town of its size?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I do. I do think so. I do think so. Because I've lived in cities. I lived in Sioux City Highway for a long time, and we were in Denver for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it comes from? Is it because the university is there, or what's the deal?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I can't figure it out.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I've always, right now, the university is what makes that town. Of course, it's a good farming area, and it's been a rich farming area. But right now, I think it's a disgrace, a town the size of Moscow, and the university is what makes it that there isn't places for those students to live. Now, I just think it's a disgrace, and I've told some of the businessmen what I think about it. And then when they come up with that billion -dollar dome up there, I said, sure, that's fine for Houston, Texas, but the town the size of Moscow, and there's no place for the students to live. Now, I happen to know at some of those dorms and those fraternity houses and the sorority houses, four years ago, there were two in the rooms last year, and this year, they've got three and four in the rooms, and there's no place for them to put their things. And over at WSU, I know two because I knew some of the boys that were over there. And the same thing at Moscow. They were sleeping in sleeping bags out in the halls in some of those places. Well, now, you can't go to school and do that.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: And as I said, don't you think that's a disgrace to the towns?

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Of course, Pullman's always building and building and building, but look what the rent is. And you know, time those students pay the rent, you as a student, you know. And I tell you, now, you see, this Lonnie, this grandson of mine, he spent five years up there. He's an architect. And he always had a scholarship, and he always worked through the summer. But even so, his folks, they had to help him all the time. He couldn't get through school. And the daughter she's teaching up in Spokane now, she had four years up there. And I tell you, it was rough for him to keep in school. But as I said, you can put up a million -dollar, billion -dollar dome, no place for the students to live.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: Now, maybe I'm not looking at it right. I don't know. No, I agree with you. I think that's a wrong priority. It really is. I know a lot of the social life in Moscow seemed to go around the churches in the early days. And I wonder if that's really part of what it is, too. That you have to be an established member of a congregation or something, too. That's right. That's right. I'll tell you one thing.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: The Mormons that come in up there, they see to it that there's a place for them to live, because with the Mormon Church and all, they see to it that there is a place for them to live. And I think it's great. But so many of the others, it's like you said, if you can't belong to the Presbyterian Church or the Methodist Church, well, the Presbyterians, they don't want anything to do with the Methodists. That's one thing I've got against Moscow. Each church, you know, is its own. And they look down on the others. And that I don't go along with. I always say, I don't care what church you belong to. There's only one God. So I don't care what church you belong to. But anyway, up there, they're like that. You'll find that in Moscow.

speaker: Beulah Dollar Herrmann
words: I'm going to have to run because what's going to happen...
